user_prompt: identify any place where actual ISRs are used
reason: documentation says we can save IRAM by
Enable CONFIG_FREERTOS_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. Provided these functions are not incorrectly used from ISRs, this option is safe to enable in all configurations.
Enable CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. Provided these functions are not incorrectly used from ISRs, this option is safe to enable in all configurations.
Enable CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_ISR_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. This option is not safe to use if the ISR ringbuf functions are used from an IRAM interrupt context, e.g., if
agent_reply: - [Buttons.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:18): real GPIO ISR, `IRAM_ATTR gpioButtonIsr`, registered with `attachInterruptArg`.

- [Buttons.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:151): ISR path calls `GpioButton::handleInterrupt()`, which calls `Hotel::noteUserActivityFromIsr()`, `millis()`, and an optional user ISR callback.
There is also `lib/Buttons/Buttons_old_code_for_reference.ino` with old ISR examples, but that looks like reference code, not the active implementation.
I did not find project-owned usage of ESP-IDF ringbuffers: no `xRingbuffer*`, `vRingbuffer*`, or `freertos/ringbuf.h` in `src`, `lib`, or `inc`.
But ESP-IDF itself has ISR ringbuffer users:
- [i2c.c](</c:/Users/frank/.platformio/packages/framework-espidf/components/driver/i2c/i2c.c:611>) uses `xRingbufferSendFromISR` and related ISR ringbuffer calls.
- deprecated RMT and ADC DMA drivers also use ringbuffer ISR functions.
- Bluetooth SPP/L2CAP use ringbuffers, mostly non-ISR paths.

`CONFIG_FREERTOS_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` looks risky for this project right now. The ESP-IDF I2S driver has IRAM DMA callbacks that call FreeRTOS `FromISR` queue APIs, and your code also has ISR critical sections. I would not enable this blindly.

`CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` is probably lower risk because your code does not directly use ESP-IDF ringbuffers from normal task context. This may be okay, especially if SPP/L2CAP are disabled.

`CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_ISR_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` is the one I would treat as unsafe until proven otherwise. Even though your code does not call ISR ringbuffer APIs, ESP-IDF I2C/RMT/ADC code does. Since this project uses I2C through M5/Arduino/device drivers, I would assume this needs caution.

user_prompt: Hotel is not allowed to fully shutdown if the logging level is higher than "error"
agent_reply: Hotel now computes a compile-time guard from:

If either one is higher than `ESP_LOG_ERROR`, the inactivity timer will no longer advance to `State::Shutdown`; it caps at `State::DimLightSleepReady` instead. So info/debug/verbose builds can still dim/light-sleep, but won’t power off under you.
- `pio run -e thefly_infolog` passes, which confirms `LOG_LOCAL_LEVEL=ESP_LOG_INFO` compiles through the guard correctly.

user_prompt: why did it reboot? I thought I had Hotel to not shutdown, the code for that looks like it's there
agent_reply: It rebooted from the light-sleep path, not the full-shutdown path.
That comes from `M5.Power.lightSleep(...)`, called here in [Hotel.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Hotel/Hotel.cpp:163):
Your `kFullShutdownAllowedByLogging` guard only affects this part:
So if logging is above error, Hotel avoids `State::Shutdown`, but `DimLightSleepReady` still allows light sleep:
The reset reason confirms it was not a clean PMIC power-off or deep-sleep wake:

A real deep-sleep wake would usually be `DEEPSLEEP_RESET`, and full PMIC power-off would look more like a fresh power-on. `RTCWDT_RTC_RESET` means the RTC watchdog reset the chip, likely because the board wedged during/around light sleep.

So: Hotel did not “fully shutdown.” It entered light sleep after inactivity, and light sleep is currently unsafe on this setup. If your intent is “when debug logging is enabled, don’t do any low-power transitions,” then `kFullShutdownAllowedByLogging` needs a companion guard for light sleep too.

The bug was that `kFullShutdownAllowedByLogging` only blocked `State::Shutdown`; light sleep was still allowed. I added `kLightSleepAllowedByLogging` and made `state_allows_light_sleep()` return `false` when logging is above error.
So debug/info builds can still dim/lower CPU, but they won’t call `M5.Power.lightSleep()` anymore, which is the path that preceded the `RTCWDT_RTC_RESET`.
I did not run a full build; `git diff --check` is clean.

The bug was that `kFullShutdownAllowedByLogging` only blocked `State::Shutdown`; light sleep was still allowed. I added `kLightSleepAllowedByLogging` and made `state_allows_light_sleep()` return `false` when logging is above error.
So debug/info builds can still dim/lower CPU, but they won’t call `M5.Power.lightSleep()` anymore, which is the path that preceded the `RTCWDT_RTC_RESET`.

user_prompt: `rst:0x10 (RTCWDT_RTC_RESET),boot:0x17 (SPI_FAST_FLASH_BOOT)` is also continuously happening as I let the device just sit now, I haven't seen this before. The clock is ticking away
I think something is wrong within Hotel, we've seen this already once, and you suspected it only happened during debug, right now I think I have debug logging off
agent_reply: The logging logic was doing what we originally asked it to do: when logging is off/error-only, Hotel allows real power saving. That means in your current build, after 30 seconds idle, it could enter `M5.Power.lightSleep()`. On this Core2 path, that appears to trigger the RTC watchdog reset you’re seeing.

I disabled only the actual light-sleep entry in [Hotel.cpp](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Hotel/Hotel.cpp). Hotel can still dim and lower CPU, but it will not call `M5.Power.lightSleep()` until we deliberately revisit that path.

commit_message: progress on bluetooth but cannot initiate connection, can handle incoming though

user_prompt: I fixed it, all I did was disable CPU frequency changing
so right now the only meaningful thing Hotel can do is dim the screen and shutdown, I think, look at my modification to it
agent_reply: I think your diagnosis is right, but I don’t see the actual CPU-disable change saved in the workspace yet.
So the `Hotel.cpp` change I can see is basically line-ending churn, not a semantic change. If your device test worked, the disabling may be in an unsaved editor buffer or a different file/build.
Conceptually, yes: with CPU frequency changes removed and `kLightSleepSupported = false`, Hotel’s meaningful jobs become:
- optionally power off after `kShutdownMs`, only when logging allows it
- track blocked state from Bluetooth/Wi-Fi/audio so it does not dim/shutdown during active work

The state names `ActiveDimAllowed`, `LightSleepReady`, and `DimLightSleepReady` become a bit stale because there is no CPU scaling and no light sleep. They mostly just mean “idle age bucket” and “brightness bucket” now.

This is a very believable root cause. Changing CPU frequency while Bluedroid is in its HFP/RFCOMM setup window could absolutely produce the “ACL connected, no SLC, timeout later” shape we were seeing.

I added `DBG_LOG_USE_SERIAL`, which routes logs through `Serial.printf` / `Serial.vprintf` and formats them like:
I also wired `platformio.ini` like this in [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini:75):

`CORE_DEBUG_LEVEL` and `LOG_LOCAL_LEVEL` stay as ESP-style values because framework headers like M5Unified read them before our `DBG_LOG_*` constants exist. Your project logging is controlled by `DBG_LOG_LOCAL_LEVEL`.

I checked the installed ESP32 SDK config and, yes, your suspicion looks right: the prebuilt package has `CONFIG_LOG_MAXIMUM_LEVEL=1`, so ESP-IDF logging above error is effectively capped. The new serial backend bypasses that.

user_prompt: is `Hotel` disabled during audio playback? I have playback active and went grocery shopping, I come back, the playback is paused but it did not shutdown due to inactivity, I think Hotel should be allowed to dim the screen during playback, and also shutdown the device if the playback is paused
agent_reply: Yes, `Hotel` was effectively disabled during playback: `AudioManager::mode() != Stopped` made file playback look like “audio active,” which forced `Hotel::Blocked` and prevented dim/shutdown.
- Recording and mic capture still fully block Hotel.
- Bluetooth and Wi-Fi still fully block Hotel.
- Active file playback can dim the screen after inactivity.
- Active file playback suppresses light sleep/shutdown while it is actually playing.
- Paused/stopped/finished playback no longer suppresses shutdown.
Build passed. `src/version.c` is still dirty separately from the build/version tooling; I did not manually edit it.

user_prompt: is `Hotel` checking the device state within itself? or is every other module telling `Hotel` what to do?
agent_reply: A bit of both, but mostly `Hotel` checks device state itself.
So for Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, recording, and mic capture, `Hotel` pulls state directly from those modules.
Other modules also tell `Hotel` about events/state that `Hotel` cannot infer cleanly:

- After my last change, `PlaybackView` calls `Hotel::setFilePlaybackPlaying(...)` because file playback state lives behind the `PlaybackView` / `FilePlayback` object, not in a global audio manager state that distinguishes playing versus paused.
So the current model is: `Hotel` owns the power policy and polls broad system state itself, while UI/input/playback publish small signals that are more local or event-like.
